body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

transcript.text: Okay. Well, I'll keep it like this. >> Cool. >> This is a real test of like what remote's really like where everybody's remote. >> It's really a shame they gave us such a small room. I know, right? When I saw the room, I thought I told him I'm going to make everybody move up. All you back row people need to move up so I can see you. [Laughter] I mean, you might as well, right? Except for Peter. Peter wants it. Do we have um do we have note takers? >> No. And we would love if somebody wants to help otherwise I'm going to do it. See, this is the problem with making me a delegate is I turn into a chair as soon as I sit here and I think, "Oh, wait. Where are my notetakers?" >> If you could bully someone into taking notes who's in the room deply appreciated by making uh violent eye contact. >> Yeah. So, we won't start until somebody takes notes, though. I will say that. Can you You're the best. >> Can we have more than one? Because then it's easier, right? Just just big things, right? Because it's recorded and everything, right? So just, >> you know, you don't have to transcribe it. We have that too, right? Sorry De, who is taking notes? We couldn't see that online. >> Empty. So, Martin Thompson. >> And then hopefully some of these other people will pitch in because we now have like 10 people. >> We're proliferating. >> 11, 12, maybe 13. Should we get started? >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. >> Um, welcome all to Keyrance at IETF123. We are your chairs. Siobhan and Pachchi joining remotely and Deb is very kindly being our in-person delegate. Thank you, Deb. Uh, this is the notewell. It governs or it talks about the ITF policies in effect such as patents or code of conduct. Please do note this well um especially note well the RFC's we have around guidelines for conduct and anti-harassment procedures. Chairs are responsible for this to make sure you have a good and productive work time at ITF but we also have the ombbuds team. So, please do get in touch if you need to raise concerns about harassment and unwelcome behavior. All right, agenda. We have a minute taker. Thank you, Martin. And for the agenda, we changed the order of presentations a little bit. Marie will be going first. And um yeah, that's the only change to the agenda. Does anyone have any other requests for change to the agenda? If not, then let's get started. Just one thing, please remember to use the meet echo tool even if you're on site uh especially for queuing because that keeps things fair for both people in the room and people online. So, please do use the the meet echo onsite tool even if you're in person. All right. Uh Marie, are you around? >> So, do you want to do your own slides? >> Do you have slides? >> You can. Siobhan, do you are you do you want me to? >> Yeah. >> You load slides and I'll pass her the clicker. >> Yeah. See this little the clicker? Oh, that's okay. We'll still give you the clicker. It's fine. >> Sorry, not share screen. Share slides. >> No, share slides. There you go. You got it. >> Okay. Ready? So, you see the clicker sitting there? >> All right. Hey everyone, my name is Mari. Um, I'm a product manager at Cloudflare. I work on privacy and emerging cryptography and going to talk a little bit about our implementations and the current state of where they're at with the drafts. All right. So, we have an early version of the draft that's been implemented. It's based on work we did last year on a non-draft compliant implementation of an auditor for key transparency that we worked on um in dropping with WhatsApp. This one uh this kind of newer implementation is uh compatible with the architecture the architecture draft zero which is an earlier draft and the protocol draft two. Um, we also want to note that there are now two parties that implement auditors. There's one implementation in Java by Signal and ours that is in Typescript. So what we would like to see moving forward is an implementation for the whole specification across an entire language. So if we could see uh you know a KT implementation and audit implementation in the same language, we think that that would make interrop testing a lot easier. Uh we'd also like to acknowledge and are very grateful for Felix from um ETH Zurich for doing some formal uh analysis for the client. I saw in the agenda that F is talking next. So that's great. What what else we would like to see uh test vectors to help facilitate implementation and interop and a clear definition clear definition of auditor data structures. So right now we have to run our auditor through uh protouff or gpc proxy. It would be nice to know or kind of standardize um that so that we can extend our auditor to audit other KT implementations in the future. Right? That's all. >> Yeah. >> Yeah. Hi, thanks for um the quick presentation update. So just to get you correctly, you implemented auditing and auditing only. >> Yes. >> And what does it mean for two auditors to interop? >> So to interrop means to um like according to the draft draft zero which is to make sure that the tree head is consistent and um consistent and append only in nature. um and to be able to check that against uh a KT implementation. Yeah, I was more wondering about the interoperability of auditors because in if I recall everything correctly and sometimes details escape me the auditor will interact with the transparency lock primarily right so auditors won't interact with each other and without a trans it seems to be I don't understand the claim without an lock implementation what does it mean for two auditors to be interoperable >> oh sorry we're we're not talking about two auditors to be entropical just for the auditor to be able to audit the key transparency implementation. >> Okay, cool. Thanks. >> Thanks. >> Just a reminder, sorry, Maria, I had a few questions. >> Oh, is he in the queue? >> Uh, no, I'm using chair privilege, but I can add myself to the queue. Um so thank you so much for um the this presentation and uh bringing you know to the ITF work that's happening um in real life. So just wondering do you did you if you're in touch with with signal folks it seems like from their code base they're making some changes to the protocol document. Could we bring those like changes to the ITF and see if there's something that we need to change in the protocol document? I guess that's my that's my first question. If you're aware of stuff that you had to change at Cloudflare or Signal had to change because it does look like there were some changes. >> Um I haven't I haven't looked in detail at the changes if the changes that Signal has made, but I'm happy to encourage them to bring those to the ITF if that's helpful. That'd be awesome. Um, and then is Cloudflare's implementation open source? I saw the link to Signals, but not to Cloudflare. >> It's not open source. Um, we're definitely considering making open source, though. >> Uh, last question. Is there also a um a client implementation in the works? Do you know or if you can talk? >> We we're not working on a client implementation. Um, and I'm actually Yeah, I don't know if I should comment or, you know, make any claims as to signal. Thanks. Uh I think Brendan's on the queue. >> Uh I just wanted to say that it is actually the kind of other way around. It's not that Signal like took the ITF draft and then made changes to it. It's that um Signal adopted a very early version of the key transparency draft and then a bunch of changes have happened to the draft in the meantime since they've implemented that in response to um difficulties that that we had with with their implementation. Um but also the signal key transparency server is open source now as well. So you can see it on their GitHub. They have the the server open source. Um and so interop I guess means that um both of those auditors can both audit the the same KT server. >> Yeah. >> Cool. Thanks. Um yeah, I mean this is super encouraging work or news for the for the working group that stuff is being deployed and being changed um in the world. >> Right. Thanks Mar. >> Okay, thanks folks. >> I think next up we have the I have we have Brendan Let me share the slides then let me pass you control. All right. Hello everyone. Uh so what I'm going to present is first of all changes to the architecture document. Um so the first sort of change to the document that we've done is originally we had text um in the document saying that users are considered to own a label if they initiate all changes to the label's value. Um and so what that means is that essentially we were saying that every time a label's value changes it was because the user went in and themselves did an update operation. Um and uh that ended up being a little bit uh too strict in practice um because in in a lot of applications KT is not actually the authoritative source for a user's public key. Um I believe the way the WhatsApp implementation works is that KT is kind of this eventually consistent thing in the background and then users will go and verify against KT but sometimes it's out of date and so you just show like a little soft error saying um couldn't verify right now please check back later. Um so uh you you have this kind of use case where the service operator is making changes to KT in the background that is unrelated to the user. Um and the what's actually happening is that the user is just being informed after the fact of the changes that happened and the user is verifying that those changes are acceptable to them. Um, so the the text was changed to users are considered to own a label if they either initiate all changes to labels value through that synchronous request response process with the server um or if they have to be informed of changes to the labels value um to sort of capture that idea that the user is responsible for the labels value but they don't actually uh make the changes to it. And of course that also is nice because it it covers use cases where you maybe have uh multiple users or multiple devices that are changing the same label. Um so one of them can make a change and then the other ones can all pick it up. Okay. And then the other kind of big thing is uh we talked a bit at last IETF about changes to contact monitoring. Um the way that it used to work was that we required users to interle requests with each other. So what would happen would be that Alice would look up Bob's public key. Um Bob would have to come online later and check that his public key is represented correctly in the log and then Alice would have to come online again after that and check that what Bob checked was correct was what she saw. Um and so the whole sort of flow was actually not secure if that interle of requests didn't happen correctly. Uh the change that we made um uh was that we added timestamps to the leaves of the log tree and the effect of that was that users could then kind of simulate this idea of interle requests or they could look at different points in the log that were created at different points in time. Um, but in terms of actual wall clock time, the users can come online whenever they want. Um, so in the section you can see that uh Alice looks at Bob's key. She waits a little bit. She does her monitoring that she has to do and then Bob can come online whenever he wants to and do his own monitoring. Um, so, uh, the the contact monitoring section of the architecture draft was updated to make it more clear that that's what was happening or that's that was updated to make it more clear that this can work that way. Um, which is nice because it's a lot more like user friendly. Um, and then we also changed third party auditing a little bit. So uh the protocol and the architecture document are are pretty strict that once the transparency log is set up and once it's configured that the configuration can never change because that introduces a lot of really difficult security issues that we don't really want to deal with. Um but uh specifically in the case of thirdparty auditing that was also a little bit too strict because um in like pop culture the way that people think about transparency logs is that the transparency log can live for a very long time and then third party auditors can kind of come and go. Third party auditors are not really ephemeral but you know they have the option to leave if they don't want to do this anymore. Um and then new ones can kind of come up over time. Um and that can introduce problems if you know number one the log is very big. If we require the auditor to start at zero that might be uh a prohibitively large amount of stuff that the auditor has to go through. Um and of course the document also supports pruning. So requiring the auditors to start at zero might not be possible if zero has been deleted in the time that the log has been alive. Um, so the the document was updated to have this sentence saying, you know, given that transparency logs are supposed to live for a very long time and that third party auditing arrangements can change over time, QT allows auditors to start auditing a log at any arbitrary point. And then in the protocol document, um, we essentially put the point at which an auditor started auditing a transparency log in that configuration. Um, so it's in the configuration with like the auditor's public key and stuff. Um and then we added some points in the protocol document where um if users are relying on a security guarantee from a third party auditor to check that the auditor has been auditing the log long enough that uh they actually get the the security property that they want. So um yeah and that verifies that users actually get the security properties they want from the auditor even if it hasn't been around you know forever. Okay. And then sort of the architecture document from the top uh sort of everything that's in it. Uh we define three interactive user operations that are like request response operations that we do with the server. Uh the first one is search which allows you to either search for the value of a specific version of a label or search for the greatest version of the label that exists. We also have this update operation which lets users change the value of a label. Um or by change the value I mean they insert a new version of a label with a new value. Um and then we also have this monitor operation which does kind of two functions. The monitor function checks that the transparency log is behaving correctly and it also if a user owns labels the monitor operation um will go through and check for any unexpected changes to uh labels that a user owns. Um, and then sort of on top of that, we defined this idea of non-interactive credentials, which is almost like a certificate where you can serialize some search operations that would normally be like request response, but you've serialized them so that you can send them to someone else and whoever received it can verify uh some kind of binding between a label and a value without doing any network access themselves. Um and then in terms of how the the transport actually operates, we have three deployment modes. The first one is contact monitoring uh which is a single party deployment mode. So anyone can kind of set up a contact monitoring log on their own. Um, the only consequence of that is that sometimes if a user looks up a label and the label was inserted very recently, the user has to remember to check back in with the log later and uh, verify that that label was not, you know, added 3 seconds before I looked it up and then deleted 3 seconds after I looked it up. Uh, so verifying that things stay in the log long enough that people can actually see that it happened. Um and then the other two modes are third-party auditing and third-party management which both um require you to have some kind of relationship with a trusted third party. Um but uh get rid of this use case where sometimes users need to maintain extra state. So with a third party auditor you have the service operator who runs the transparency log um and then the auditors kind of off to the side and just verifying the log is operating correctly. with thirdparty management. You have a manager who is basically running the entire transparency log u and then the service operator does some kind of lightweight things to keep this third party in check. And so those are all of the sort of binding guidance that we provide in the architecture document. Um we also provide just like advisory text on um how to combine multiple logs and that goes also into how to migrate from one log to another. We talk a lot about explaining in a in a kind of a user-friendly way the security and the privacy properties of the protocol. Um we talk about pruning the log for efficiency. So getting rid of kind of old things that you don't want to look at anymore versus pruning to comply with privacy law where that's going to be a little bit more aggressive because you have to get rid of the data everywhere right now versus with um uh pruning for efficiency you maybe can leave things around a little bit more. And then also we talk about how to actually structure an application's data in KT um so that users or not users applications can convert you know the security properties of KT into broader security properties for their application and all of that is really just to help people use KT successfully because it kind of is a complicated protocol. Um but yeah that's it for the architecture document. Are there any questions on architecture? Hearing none, I'm going to move on to changes to the protocol document. Uh so the kind of exciting thing that we did in the protocol document is that we defined how third party auditing works. Now uh so the way that it works is every time the transparency log adds a new leaf to the log tree uh it has to send this auditor update structure to the third party auditor and this auditor update structure contains the timestamp of the new leaf of the log tree. It also contains all of the prefix leaves that were added to the prefix tree with this new log entry and then it also contains all of the prefix leaves that were removed from the prefix tree in this new version. Um and then it contains a lookup proof which allows the auditor to uh number one compute the previous version of the prefix tree uh the prefix tree root hash before the stuff was added and removed and then allows the auditor to compute the new prefix tree root hash after all of the stuff has been added and removed. Um and um what the auditor does when it receives that is that you know number one it verifies that the time stamp is greater than or equal to the time stamp of the immediately previous log entry. Um and it also verifies that for all of the leaves of the prefix tree that were removed. Uh all of those values had been in the log long enough that uh they were all published in at least one distinguished log entry before they got removed. And the purpose of that is to guarantee the kind of same security property we get with contact monitoring which is uh you know data doesn't have to be in the log forever but it has to be in the log long enough that someone looking for it would be able to find it easily. Uh so that's um how how third party auditing works. We also define how third party management works and this is a little bit simpler. So what happens with thirdparty management is that the service operator is kind of like a middleman between access to the third party manager. Um so whenever users want to submit an update request, they take that update request and they submit it to the service operator first. The service operator does like access control and all of that and then it wraps that update request in this manager update request structure which just contains the user's original update request in it and then it also contains a signature from the service operator. Um and yeah and so then whenever users actually do like a search result um the the signature from the service operator that we provided here just gets immediately provided back and users verify that um whatever the third party manager is giving them back as the value for this label uh was actually like authorized to be added to the transparency log by the service operator. So that's what that signature conveys. And then we also added a registry of cypher suites. This is probably the most exciting although um we'll dig a little bit into it. Um so the first cipher suite that we have defined here is a 128 bit security level cipher suite where the hash function we use is shot 256 and then for signatures we use ECDSA P256 and for the VRF we use the P256 VRF and then the second cy suite is sort of the same thing Shaw 256 for hash functions and then we use uh ED DSA for signatures and the Edwards 25519 VR ARF or the the VRF. Um, and that's it. Just two cipher suites. Um, it would have been nicer to have maybe like a a 256- bit security level or like potentially something that's postquantum secure. Um, but we we're limited because the CFRG in RFC 9381 has only specified two elliptic or VRFs and they're both at the 128 bit security level. So that's just kind of all that we have to pull from from the CFRG. So getting more cypher suites would likely require pushing a document through CFRG and that is maybe out of scope for now in terms of workload. Um and then adding new entries to the to the the registry for cipher suites. Uh the the policy there that we have set is specific specification required. um and specification required. The rule there is that to register a new code point in the registry. Uh you have to have a specification. The specification has to be um public and it also has to be permanent. So it can't change. And that's a lot of times in other working groups interpreted to be that you need an RFC to have a specification required code point, but that's not true. you can point to I believe other standards bodies or like something on GitHub potentially um because the rules are just that it has to be permanent and public um yeah CFRG would be a lot of delay I think um we can register new code points afterwards if it's important um and then the protocol document from the top as well so uh the protocol document defines two main tree structures that we work with. The first is called a log tree and a log tree is uh what's called a left balanced binary tree which is this property that every left sub tree is the largest possible balanced binary tree. Um and then we also define a prefix tree which is internally what's called a try. And a try is a tree structure with this property that um every node represents some prefix and then every node's left child represents that same prefix plus a zero bit and every node's right child represents that same prefix plus a one bit. And um all of the the search keys stored below a specific node all share its prefix. Um so those are the two kind of basic tree structures we define. Uh those are combined into what's called a combined tree. So uh at the top level the combined tree is just a log tree and then every leaf of the log tree contains uh first of all a time stamp of when the leaf was added and then also the root hash of the current version of the prefix tree. Um and in the prefix tree, every time we change the prefix tree, what we're doing is um we're inserting new label value mappings that we want stored in the transparency log. So um over time as we insert new values for labels, the prefix tree kind of grows like this where at the very beginning we're just going to have the space set of mapping label A to value B and then over time we'll add like C to D and then it it grows incrementally over time. Um, and of course you can look back in the log tree and see what the root hash of the prefix tree was at different points in time. Uh, so for working with this combinary structure to make it like useful, uh, we define two kind of subconcepts. The first one is called the binary ladder. A binary ladder is just a series of lookups in a specific version of the prefix tree. Um, and the purpose of these lookups is to determine whether or not a given version of a label exists or not. So, I would do these lookups to determine if um I'm looking for some specific version. I would do those lookups to determine if that version existed yet or not as of whatever version of the prefix tree. And then we also have this idea of uh distinguished log entries. So, distinguished log entries are uh specific log entries uh leaf is a log tree that are chosen at a timebased interval. So new leaves of the log tree can be added kind of at whatever rate you need to add them. Um but we will go through and pick according to an algorithm um roughly one log entry that was added per every time interval. So if the time interval is one day, we'll go through and pick a log entry roughly one per day. And the purpose of that is to align consistency checks between users. Uh so users can all have this one log entry per day that we can all check as correct. Um, and then in terms of how search works, like I said, we support fixed version search and greatest version search. The way that fixed version search works is essentially it's just a binary search through all of the leaves of the log tree and we're binary searching for the first leaf of the log tree where the prefix tree contains the uh label version pair that we're looking for. And then um the way that we decide at each log leaf whether or not we should go left or right in our binary search is we use a binary ladder. Um so we go to say the middle log entry. We use a binary ladder from the the prefix tree in that u log entry uh to decide whether the version we care about exists or not. And if it does we go right. If it doesn't we go left. Um and then also for greatest version search this is a little bit simpler. Uh the purpose of this is just to find the greatest version of a label that exists. So we start at the rightmost distinguished log entry which is the the most recent distinguished log entry that was created. Um and we find the greatest version of the label that exists in that distinguished log entry. So that's the the greatest version of the label that exists as of the newest distinguished log entry. Um, and then we kind of binary search to the right. Not really binary searching for something specific, just moving to the rightmost log entry. Um, repeating that kind of step, finding the greatest version of the label that exists in each of these different log entries. And the purpose of that is just to ensure that we intersect with um if uh a new version of the label was inserted since the last distinguished log entry was created. Uh this guarantees that we'll intersect with whatever the label owner is going to check uh for correctness as well. And then the way that monitoring works is um whenever we have previously looked up some version of a label and it was to the right of the rightmost distinguished log entry. So that means it was inserted very recently. Um we just check uh whether any kind of fixed version or greatest version search for that same version of the label would still converge back to what we saw originally. So it's kind of the um inverse of the search operation which is we're saying that okay with the new log entries which have been added since the last time I monitored the log have those new log entries been created correctly such that if someone searches for the same version of the label that I saw previously will they still go back to what I saw originally? Um, and also if the user owns some labels, um, what the user does is they do this kind of depth first search for distinguished log entries and they walk all of the distinguished log entries which has been created since the last time they monitored. And in each of those log entries, we find the greatest version of the label that exists and we check that that's what we expect. So essentially, we're just checking that um, nothing has been inserted for our label that we are not aware of that we didn't authorize. So yeah and uh that's roughly how the protocol document works. So um this is my last slide. So before Montreal next ITF app the changes that I would like to have to the protocol document are I would like to add an explicit update operation because right now update is basically just search. Um, but that doesn't really work with the automated updates idea that I talked about at the beginning where um, users are not always initiating the update operation themselves. Um, and then, you know, other sort of random minor tweaks and bug fixes. Um, I still have in my mind from last IETF, we talked about adding a prefix to all of the structures that are assigned just for like domain separation. Um, and I'm also planning to do a fresh implementation because like we talked about uh uh some imple implementations are getting published right now which is really exciting but they're all of relatively old versions of the protocol which is um what it is you know because we implemented the the previous version of the protocol and then we decided to change the protocol to address things that didn't work very well and so now we have to go back and implement the protocol again but I would like to do that in the next couple of months but um Yeah, the the political document and the architecture document I both think are getting fairly stable in the sense that I don't have a lot of work that I want to do on them. Um, so please, you know, review it and also please share your thoughts on the mailing list or or here. Um, yeah, that's all I've got. I was waiting to see if someone else was joining to give other folks a chance, but um I'll just go first. So um thanks Brendan. Just wondering uh like you said if the implementation that you know of a little bit behind in terms of the or compared to the protocol document, do you have a sense for whether the implementation would want to move on to the latest burdens like is that like do you know of that or do you know if it's just a question of implementation effort or is there something you know like fundamental that in the changes that would prevent someone from um adopting the changes even if it's like bug fixes because it's like doesn't match the deployment or something like that. >> Um I believe that's on the table. Yes. Um, I obviously don't want to make promises, but um, yeah, I don't really see a problem with that. I think Marie probably left. She said she had a conflict. Yep. Oh, yes, she did a little bit earlier. Sorry. >> Do we have more questions for Brandon? >> Yeah. Hi. Thanks, Brandon. um when you presented the service operator update where every for the third party management of the log where every update is signed. I was wondering um whether we should also allow for other mechanisms to do to uh to authenticate these requests for example mutual TLS authentication on the transport layer and then you don't sign each update but you have um a secure channel. It seems like this falls a bit out of the scope of the core protocol model. So we might want to be a bit flexible there. What do you think? >> I'm not totally sure how TLS would work. Um because the point of this is that the service operator signs it. It goes to the third party manager. Um and then later that signature is proxied back from the third party manager to users whenever they do search requests. So the purpose is to sort of get that through line of service the service operator signed it when it was added and then later when I'm looking it up I can see that that signature is still there. So the service operator must have signed it in the past. Um we can potentially have it signed um by a different party. >> Yeah. Okay. I in I got the security guarantees you want to establish from the signature differently. So what you say makes sense. I guess this then has to do with which collusion of parties you want to accept or not accept, right? If you treat >> if you treat the third party operator and the lock owner or like the so the third party management and the like the messaging application for example as one party then I guess you could even drop signatures entirely >> potentially. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Thanks. >> Thank you. Any other questions from Brendan? >> All right, I think we can move on. Thanks a lot, Brendon. All right, next up we have Phoenix. No clickers required. Yeah. Hi. Um, this is more presentation for the record because I didn't want to let this meeting pass without saying anything. So, I only have a single slide. um as has been uh shared by Mary, I've well we've been working on a verification of the draft in a um verified reference implementation. The repository is linked here again. Uh I'm collaborating with this with Leonard Rquind who's also working at ETHZurich and with Cororey Meers and Jonathan Oiland. Um you may know some of these names, they're also in chat. Sorry for not mentioning that on the slide. I unfortunately have nothing definitive to announce. Uh we are progressing in both implementation and verification but unfortunately the progress is slower than hoped and the reason is a mixture of uh verifying the ident identified properties is more challenging than we thought and the team members have conflicting projects and cannot uh put in as much time as we had hoped. So now you can be the judge of how how much percentage you assign to each of these uh reasons but yeah it falls in in between of the two. Um nevertheless I think that the verification efforts we have put in so far have provided value. We didn't find any security issues in the draft so far but many small quirks that you find while implementing the draft. And I think yeah Brenton and I were always very quick to address them. So I think all of them should be addressed in the current draft. Maybe there are some minor um ones left open and we intend to proceed as planned. I'm trying to figure out how to allocate the necessary resources to this project. Um and that's all I have to say on this point. Are there any questions? Good. Um, yeah, I'll be around still. That was my update on the key transparency verification. Thank you. >> Cool. Um, thanks Rex. We don't have much else. Do folks have questions, comments, any last minute presentations? Rajie, any thoughts? >> No, I think we are on the right track. Um, I have a question for Brendan though. Brendan, you mentioned the updates you want to make before the next ITF. Um, And those are just protocol document updates. Are you planning to make any to the architecture document as well? >> Uh I do plan to add some more um just basically non-binding sort of advisory text to the architecture document. Um so one thing that I want to talk about is uh with contact monitoring there's a requirement that users maintain state. I want to kind of quantify how important maintaining state is given that a lot of applications, you know, people reboot their phone constantly or like reset their phone or whatever. I want to help I want to see if I can quantify the security impacts of that. >> Okay, thanks Brendan. Sounds good. >> Yeah. the I I think we are getting to a point where after next idea we'll be closer to a last call probably. >> Uh but we'll see. >> Yeah. >> Deb, anything from you? >> Hi De, can you hear us? >> Oh, yes. I'm sorry. >> Just was just asking you if there's anything from you. Can you say one more time >> anything from your site? >> No. >> Or should we close out the meeting? >> No. No. Although I do think you can um you know if if Brendan's not getting is Brendan getting reviews of both of these drafts are they or is he just doing all this work by himself or other people reviewing or how does this work? Because we start need to start getting re people to review it if that's not happening. Oh, Brendan, do you have a um request for review or if you feel like there are things that the working group should be looking at more? Uh so what I'm going to present is first
of all changes to the architecture
document. Um so the first sort of change
to the document that we've done is
originally we had text um in the
document saying that users are
considered to own a label if they
initiate all changes to the label's
value. Um and so what that means is that
essentially we were saying that every
time a label's value changes it was
because the user went in and themselves
did an update operation. Um and uh that
ended up being a little bit uh too
strict in practice um because in in a
lot of applications KT is not actually
the authoritative source for a user's
public key. Um I believe the way the
WhatsApp implementation works is that KT
is kind of this eventually consistent
thing in the background and then users
will go and verify against KT but
sometimes it's out of date and so you
just show like a little soft error
saying um couldn't verify right now
please check back later.
Um so uh you you have this kind of use
case where the service operator is
making changes to KT in the background
that is unrelated to the user. Um and
the what's actually happening is that
the user is just being informed after
the fact of the changes that happened
and the user is verifying that those
changes are acceptable to them. Um, so
the the text was changed to users are
considered to own a label if they either
initiate all changes to labels value
through that synchronous request
response process with the server um or
if they have to be informed of changes
to the labels value um to sort of
capture that idea that the user is
responsible for the labels value but
they don't actually uh make the changes
to it. And of course that also is nice
because it it covers use cases where you
maybe have uh multiple users or multiple
devices that are changing the same
label. Um so one of them can make a
change and then the other ones can all
pick it up.
Okay. And then the other kind of big
thing is uh we talked a bit at last IETF
about changes to contact monitoring. Um
the way that it used to work was that we
required users to interle requests with
each other. So what would happen would
be that Alice would look up Bob's public
key. Um Bob would have to come online
later and check that his public key is
represented correctly in the log and
then Alice would have to come online
again after that and check that what Bob
checked was correct was what she saw. Um
and so the whole sort of flow was
actually not secure if that interle of
requests didn't happen correctly. Uh the
change that we made um uh was that we
added timestamps to the leaves of the
log tree and the effect of that was that
users could then kind of simulate this
idea of interle requests or they could
look at different points in the log that
were created at different points in
time. Um, but in terms of actual wall
clock time, the users can come online
whenever they want. Um, so in the
section you can see that uh Alice looks
at Bob's key. She waits a little bit.
She does her monitoring that she has to
do and then Bob can come online whenever
he wants to and do his own monitoring.
Um, so, uh, the the contact monitoring
section of the architecture draft was
updated to make it more clear that
that's what was happening or that's that
was updated to make it more clear that
this can work that way. Um, which is
nice because it's a lot more like user
friendly.
Um, and then we also changed third party
auditing a little bit. So uh the
protocol and the architecture document
are are pretty strict that once the
transparency log is set up and once it's
configured that the configuration can
never change because that introduces a
lot of really difficult security issues
that we don't really want to deal with.
Um but uh specifically in the case of
thirdparty auditing that was also a
little bit too strict because um in like
pop culture the way that people think
about transparency logs is that the
transparency log can live for a very
long time and then third party auditors
can kind of come and go. Third party
auditors are not really ephemeral but
you know they have the option to leave
if they don't want to do this anymore.
Um and then new ones can kind of come up
over time. Um and that can introduce
problems if you know number one the log
is very big. If we require the auditor
to start at zero that might be uh a
prohibitively large amount of stuff that
the auditor has to go through. Um and of
course the document also supports
pruning. So requiring the auditors to
start at zero might not be possible if
zero has been deleted in the time that
the log has been alive. Um, so the the
document was updated to have this
sentence saying, you know, given that
transparency logs are supposed to live
for a very long time and that third
party auditing arrangements can change
over time, QT allows auditors to start
auditing a log at any arbitrary point.
And then in the protocol document, um,
we essentially put the point at which an
auditor started auditing a transparency
log in that configuration. Um, so it's
in the configuration with like the
auditor's public key and stuff. Um and
then we added some points in the
protocol document where um if users are
relying on a security guarantee from a
third party auditor to check that the
auditor has been auditing the log long
enough that uh they actually get the the
security property that they want. So um
yeah and that verifies that users
actually get the security properties
they want from the auditor even if it
hasn't been around you know forever.
Okay. And then sort of the architecture
document from the top uh sort of
everything that's in it. Uh we define
three interactive user operations that
are like request response operations
that we do with the server. Uh the first
one is search which allows you to either
search for the value of a specific
version of a label or search for the
greatest version of the label that
exists. We also have this update
operation which lets users change the
value of a label. Um or by change the
value I mean they insert a new version
of a label with a new value. Um and then
we also have this monitor operation
which does kind of two functions. The
monitor function checks that the
transparency log is behaving correctly
and it also if a user owns labels the
monitor operation um
will go through and check for any
unexpected changes to uh labels that a
user owns. Um, and then sort of on top
of that, we defined this idea of
non-interactive credentials, which is
almost like a certificate where you can
serialize some search operations that
would normally be like request response,
but you've serialized them so that you
can send them to someone else and
whoever received it can verify uh some
kind of binding between a label and a
value without doing any network access
themselves. Um
and then in terms of how the the
transport actually operates, we have
three deployment modes. The first one is
contact monitoring uh which is a single
party deployment mode. So anyone can
kind of set up a contact monitoring log
on their own. Um, the only consequence
of that is that sometimes if a user
looks up a label and the label was
inserted very recently, the user has to
remember to check back in with the log
later and uh, verify that that label was
not, you know, added 3 seconds before I
looked it up and then deleted 3 seconds
after I looked it up. Uh, so verifying
that things stay in the log long enough
that people can actually see that it
happened. Um and then the other two
modes are third-party auditing and
third-party management which both um
require you to have some kind of
relationship with a trusted third party.
Um but uh get rid of this use case where
sometimes users need to maintain extra
state. So with a third party auditor you
have the service operator who runs the
transparency log um and then the
auditors kind of off to the side and
just verifying the log is operating
correctly. with thirdparty management.
You have a manager who is basically
running the entire transparency log u
and then the service operator does some
kind of lightweight things to keep this
third party in check. And so those are
all of the sort of binding guidance that
we provide in the architecture document.
Um we also provide just like advisory
text on um how to combine multiple logs
and that goes also into how to migrate
from one log to another. We talk a lot
about explaining in a in a kind of a
user-friendly way the security and the
privacy properties of the protocol. Um
we talk about pruning the log for
efficiency. So getting rid of kind of
old things that you don't want to look
at anymore versus pruning to comply with
privacy law where that's going to be a
little bit more aggressive because you
have to get rid of the data everywhere
right now versus with um uh pruning for
efficiency you maybe can leave things
around a little bit more. And then also
we talk about how to actually structure
an application's data in KT
um so that users or not users
applications can convert you know the
security properties of KT into broader
security properties for their
application and all of that is really
just to help people use KT successfully
because it kind of is a complicated
protocol. Um but yeah that's it for the
architecture document. Are there any
questions on architecture?
Hearing none, I'm going to move on to
changes to the protocol document.
Uh so the kind of exciting thing that we
did in the protocol document is that we
defined how third party auditing works.
Now uh so the way that it works is every
time the transparency log adds a new
leaf to the log tree uh it has to send
this auditor update structure to the
third party auditor and this auditor
update structure contains the timestamp
of the new leaf of the log tree. It also
contains all of the prefix leaves that
were added to the prefix tree with this
new log entry and then it also contains
all of the prefix leaves that were
removed from the prefix tree in this new
version. Um and then it contains a
lookup proof which allows the auditor to
uh number one compute the previous
version of the prefix tree uh the prefix
tree root hash before the stuff was
added and removed and then allows the
auditor to compute the new prefix tree
root hash after all of the stuff has
been added and removed. Um and um what
the auditor does when it receives that
is that you know number one it verifies
that the time stamp is greater than or
equal to the time stamp of the
immediately previous log entry. Um and
it also verifies that for all of the
leaves of the prefix tree that were
removed. Uh all of those values had been
in the log long enough that uh they were
all published in at least one
distinguished log entry before they got
removed. And the purpose of that is to
guarantee the kind of same security
property we get with contact monitoring
which is uh you know data doesn't have
to be in the log forever but it has to
be in the log long enough that someone
looking for it would be able to find it
easily. Uh so that's um how how third
party auditing works.
We also define how third party
management works and this is a little
bit simpler. So what happens with
thirdparty management is that the
service operator is kind of like a
middleman between access to the third
party manager. Um so whenever users want
to submit an update request, they take
that update request and they submit it
to the service operator first. The
service operator does like access
control and all of that and then it
wraps that update request in this
manager update request structure which
just contains the user's original update
request in it and then it also contains
a signature from the service operator.
Um and yeah and so then whenever users
actually do like a search result um the
the signature from the service operator
that we provided here just gets
immediately provided back and users
verify that um whatever the third party
manager is giving them back as the value
for this label uh was actually like
authorized to be added to the
transparency log by the service
operator. So that's what that signature
conveys.
And then we also added a registry of
cypher suites. This is probably the most
exciting although um we'll dig a little
bit into it. Um so the first cipher
suite that we have defined here is a 128
bit security level cipher suite where
the hash function we use is shot 256 and
then for signatures we use ECDSA P256
and for the VRF we use the P256 VRF and
then the second cy suite is sort of the
same thing Shaw 256 for hash functions
and then we use uh ED DSA for signatures
and the Edwards 25519 VR ARF or the the
VRF. Um, and that's it. Just two cipher
suites. Um, it would have been nicer to
have maybe like a a 256- bit security
level or like potentially something
that's postquantum secure. Um, but we
we're limited because the CFRG in RFC
has only specified two elliptic or VRFs
and they're both at the 128 bit security
level. So that's just kind of all that
we have to pull from from the CFRG. So
getting more cypher suites would likely
require pushing a document through CFRG
and that is maybe out of scope for now
in terms of workload. Um and then adding
new entries to the to the the registry
for cipher suites. Uh the the policy
there that we have set is specific
specification required.
um and specification required. The rule
there is that to register a new code
point in the registry. Uh you have to
have a specification. The specification
has to be um public and it also has to
be permanent. So it can't change. And
that's a lot of times in other working
groups interpreted to be that you need
an RFC to have a specification required
code point, but that's not true. you can
point to I believe other standards
bodies or like something on GitHub
potentially um because the rules are
just that it has to be permanent and
public um
yeah CFRG would be a lot of delay I
think um we can register new code points
afterwards if it's important
um
and then the protocol document from the
top as well so uh the protocol document
defines two main tree structures that we
work with. The first is called a log
tree and a log tree is uh what's called
a left balanced binary tree which is
this property that every left sub tree
is the largest possible balanced binary
tree. Um and then we also define a
prefix tree which is internally what's
called a try. And a try is a tree
structure with this property that um
every node represents some prefix and
then every node's left child represents
that same prefix plus a zero bit and
every node's right child represents that
same prefix plus a one bit. And um all
of the the search keys stored below a
specific node all share its prefix. Um
so those are the two kind of basic tree
structures we define.
Uh those are combined into what's called
a combined tree. So uh at the top level
the combined tree is just a log tree and
then every leaf of the log tree contains
uh first of all a time stamp of when the
leaf was added and then also the root
hash of the current version of the
prefix tree.
Um and in the prefix tree, every time we
change the prefix tree, what we're doing
is um we're inserting new label value
mappings that we want stored in the
transparency log. So um over time as we
insert new values for labels, the prefix
tree kind of grows like this where at
the very beginning we're just going to
have the space set of mapping label A to
value B and then over time we'll add
like C to D and then it it grows
incrementally over time. Um, and of
course you can look back in the log tree
and see what the root hash of the prefix
tree was at different points in time.
Uh, so for working with this combinary
structure to make it like useful, uh, we
define two kind of subconcepts. The
first one is called the binary ladder. A
binary ladder is just a series of
lookups in a specific version of the
prefix tree. Um, and the purpose of
these lookups is to determine whether or
not a given version of a label exists or
not. So, I would do these lookups to
determine if um I'm looking for some
specific version. I would do those
lookups to determine if that version
existed yet or not as of whatever
version of the prefix tree. And then we
also have this idea of uh distinguished
log entries. So, distinguished log
entries are uh specific log entries uh
leaf is a log tree that are chosen at a
timebased interval. So new leaves of the
log tree can be added kind of at
whatever rate you need to add them. Um
but we will go through and pick
according to an algorithm um roughly one
log entry that was added per every time
interval. So if the time interval is one
day, we'll go through and pick a log
entry roughly one per day. And the
purpose of that is to align consistency
checks between users. Uh so users can
all have this one log entry per day that
we can all check as correct. Um,
and then in terms of how search works,
like I said, we support fixed version
search and greatest version search. The
way that fixed version search works is
essentially it's just a binary search
through all of the leaves of the log
tree and we're binary searching for the
first leaf of the log tree where the
prefix tree contains the uh label
version pair that we're looking for. And
then um the way that we decide at each
log leaf whether or not we should go
left or right in our binary search is we
use a binary ladder. Um so we go to say
the middle log entry. We use a binary
ladder from the the prefix tree in that
u log entry uh to decide whether the
version we care about exists or not. And
if it does we go right. If it doesn't we
go left. Um and then also for greatest
version search this is a little bit
simpler. Uh the purpose of this is just
to find the greatest version of a label
that exists. So we start at the
rightmost distinguished log entry which
is the the most recent distinguished log
entry that was created. Um and we find
the greatest version of the label that
exists in that distinguished log entry.
So that's the the greatest version of
the label that exists as of the newest
distinguished log entry. Um, and then we
kind of binary search to the right. Not
really binary searching for something
specific, just moving to the rightmost
log entry. Um, repeating that kind of
step, finding the greatest version of
the label that exists in each of these
different log entries. And the purpose
of that is just to ensure that we
intersect with um if uh a new version of
the label was inserted since the last
distinguished log entry was created. Uh
this guarantees that we'll intersect
with whatever the label owner is going
to check uh for correctness as well.
And then the way that monitoring works
is um whenever we have previously looked
up some version of a label and it was to
the right of the rightmost distinguished
log entry. So that means it was inserted
very recently. Um we just check uh
whether any kind of fixed version or
greatest version search for that same
version of the label would still
converge back to what we saw originally.
So it's kind of the um inverse of the
search operation which is we're saying
that okay with the new log entries which
have been added since the last time I
monitored the log have those new log
entries been created correctly such that
if someone searches for the same version
of the label that I saw previously will
they still go back to what I saw
originally? Um, and also if the user
owns some labels, um, what the user does
is they do this kind of depth first
search for distinguished log entries and
they walk all of the distinguished log
entries which has been created since the
last time they monitored. And in each of
those log entries, we find the greatest
version of the label that exists and we
check that that's what we expect. So
essentially, we're just checking that
um, nothing has been inserted for our
label that we are not aware of that we
didn't authorize. So yeah
and uh that's roughly how the protocol
document works. So um this is my last
slide. So before Montreal next ITF app
the changes that I would like to have to
the protocol document are I would like
to add an explicit update operation
because right now update is basically
just search. Um, but that doesn't really
work with the automated updates idea
that I talked about at the beginning
where um, users are not always
initiating the update operation
themselves. Um, and then, you know,
other sort of random minor tweaks and
bug fixes. Um, I still have in my mind
from last IETF, we talked about adding a
prefix to all of the structures that are
